up.method: experimentally determined uptake rate used as model constraint at mu=0.25/h; OD600->DCW via 1 OD600=0.448 gDCW/L
experimentally determined oxygen uptake rate at mu=0.25/h

growth_records.cite: Park JM et al. Genome-scale reconstruction and in silico analysis of the Ralstonia eutropha H16 for polyhydroxyalkanoate synthesis, lithoautotrophic growth, and 2-methyl citric acid production. BMC Systems Biology (2011). DOI:10.1186/1752-0509-5-101. [PMC3154180]
growth_records.snippet: The oxygen and D-fructose uptake rates of 4.6 and 2.6 mmol gDCW-1 h-1, respectively, were used as constraints at the growth rate of 0.25 h-1 ... 1 OD600 = 0.448 gDCW/L
growth_records.notes: Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.25=2.77 h. Experimental heterotrophic aerobic growth on MR medium + 20 g/L D-fructose at 30C. Measured qS(fructose)=2.6 and qO2=4.6 mmol/gDCW/h at mu=0.25/h. Chemostat runs at D=0.05,0.07,0.10/h (mu=D at steady state) reported but per-dilution fructose-uptake values are given only in Additional file 4/figure, not in article text, so not tabulated here. Lithoautotrophic (H2/CO2/O2) growth rates in the paper are in silico predictions compared against experimental data cited from ref [35] (another paper), not measured here.

sec.method: volumetric productivity reported in review Table 2

growth_records.cite: Jiang et al. Carbon Sources for Polyhydroxyalkanoates and an Integrated Biorefinery. International Journal of Molecular Sciences (2016). DOI:10.3390/ijms17071157. [PMC4964529]
growth_records.notes: REVIEW secondary value (Table 2, ref [7], marked commercial + large-scale). CDW 164 g/L, 73.8% P3HB, productivity 2.42 g/L/h, yield 0.32-0.48 g P3HB/g glucose. No growth rate, no per-gDW uptake, no medium composition/temp/pH in review. Volumetric productivity = PHA content / fermentation time (per review footnote c).

growth_records.cite: Jiang et al. Carbon Sources for Polyhydroxyalkanoates and an Integrated Biorefinery. International Journal of Molecular Sciences (2016). DOI:10.3390/ijms17071157. [PMC4964529]
growth_records.notes: REVIEW secondary value (Table 2, ref [66], commercial). CDW 120 g/L, 62.5% P3HB, 0.96 g/L/h, yield 0.72-0.76 g/g oil (high yield reflects high carbon content of oils).

growth_records.cite: Jiang et al. Carbon Sources for Polyhydroxyalkanoates and an Integrated Biorefinery. International Journal of Molecular Sciences (2016). DOI:10.3390/ijms17071157. [PMC4964529]
growth_records.snippet: Waste frying oil | R. eutropha | 10.4 | 36.5 | P3HB | 0.14 | 0.19-0.34 [86]. Morais et al. achieved a productivity of 0.14 g PHB/L/h and a yield of 0.14 g PHB/g waste oil.
growth_records.notes: REVIEW secondary value (Table 2 + text, ref [86], Morais et al., 5-L batch). CDW 10.4 g/L, 36.5% P3HB, 0.14 g/L/h. Yield reported as 0.19-0.34 g/g in Table 2 but 0.14 g/g in text -- both recorded verbatim (discrepancy within the review).

growth_records.cite: Jiang et al. Carbon Sources for Polyhydroxyalkanoates and an Integrated Biorefinery. International Journal of Molecular Sciences (2016). DOI:10.3390/ijms17071157. [PMC4964529]
growth_records.snippet: The recombinant R. eutropha when grown in a 5-L batch fermentor produced an average of 0.0046 g/L/h of PHA, a relatively very low productivity for PHA production.
growth_records.notes: REVIEW in-text secondary value (Park et al. [21]). Recombinant R. eutropha on sucrose, 5-L batch, 0.0046 g PHA/L/h (very low).
